words.shortDefinition: The protocol browsers and servers use to exchange data on the web.
words.longDefinition: HyperText Transfer Protocol defines how clients request resources and servers respond. It is stateless and text-based. HTTP/1.1 introduced persistent connections; HTTP/2 added multiplexing; HTTP/3 runs over QUIC for lower latency.
words.techContext: Every web API call is HTTP. Understanding methods, status codes, headers, and keep-alive is essential for debugging network issues.
words.exampleSentence: The API was returning HTTP 200 even on errors, making client-side error handling nearly impossible.
words.etymology: coined by Tim Berners-Lee at CERN in 1989 alongside HTML and the first web server.

codeExample.code:
GET /users/42 HTTP/1.1
Host: api.example.com
Authorization: Bearer <token>

→ HTTP/1.1 200 OK
→ Content-Type: application/json

words.shortDefinition: An architectural style for APIs that uses HTTP methods and resource-based URLs.
words.longDefinition: Representational State Transfer defines six constraints — statelessness, uniform interface, client-server separation, cacheability, layered system, and optional code on demand. A RESTful API maps CRUD operations to HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) on noun-based endpoints.
words.techContext: REST is the dominant API style for web services. Its stateless nature makes it horizontally scalable by default.
words.exampleSentence: We designed the API following REST conventions so each resource had a predictable URL and the correct HTTP method.
words.etymology: coined by Roy Fielding in his 2000 PhD dissertation at UC Irvine.

codeExample.code:
GET    /articles        # list
POST   /articles        # create
GET    /articles/42     # read
PUT    /articles/42     # update
DELETE /articles/42     # delete

words.shortDefinition: A defined contract that lets one program communicate with another.
words.longDefinition: An Application Programming Interface specifies how software components interact: what operations are available, what inputs they accept, and what outputs they return. Web APIs use HTTP; library APIs expose functions; OS APIs expose system calls.
words.techContext: APIs are everywhere — you consume them (third-party services) and produce them (your backend). Good API design is about clarity and stability.
words.exampleSentence: We versioned the API so breaking changes in v2 didn't affect clients still using v1.
words.etymology: first appeared in IBM documentation in the 1960s; popularised in web development in the 2000s.

words.shortDefinition: A specific URL at which an API is accessible and responds to requests.
words.longDefinition: An endpoint is the combination of an HTTP method and a URL path that maps to a handler function. Well-designed endpoints use plural nouns (/users), avoid verbs (/getUser), and nest resources logically (/users/42/orders).
words.techContext: Endpoint design decisions (naming, versioning, nesting depth) are hard to change once clients depend on them.
words.exampleSentence: The mobile team was hitting the wrong endpoint because the staging URL had not been updated in the config.
words.etymology: compound of end + point; adopted from networking (cable termination) to mean an API access point.

words.shortDefinition: A browser security mechanism that controls which origins can make requests to a different domain.
words.longDefinition: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing lets servers declare which external domains may access their resources via HTTP headers. Browsers enforce the same-origin policy by default; CORS headers opt out of that restriction. Preflight OPTIONS requests are sent for non-simple requests.
words.techContext: CORS errors are one of the most common frustrations in web development. The fix is always server-side — add the right Access-Control-Allow-Origin header.
words.exampleSentence: The frontend was blocked by a CORS error because the backend hadn't whitelisted the staging domain.
words.etymology: acronym coined by W3C in the 2009 CORS specification draft.

words.shortDefinition: A small piece of data a server sends to the browser to persist state across requests.
words.longDefinition: Cookies are key-value pairs stored in the browser and automatically sent with every matching HTTP request. They carry session IDs, preferences, and tracking identifiers. Important attributes: HttpOnly (no JS access), Secure (HTTPS only), SameSite (CSRF protection), and Max-Age (expiry).
words.techContext: Session cookies expire when the browser closes; persistent cookies last until Max-Age. HttpOnly and Secure flags are security essentials.
words.exampleSentence: We set the session cookie with HttpOnly and Secure flags to prevent XSS from stealing it.
words.etymology: named by Lou Montulli at Netscape (1994) after the computing term 'magic cookie' — a token passed between programs.

words.shortDefinition: An HTTP callback that lets one service notify another when an event occurs.
words.longDefinition: Webhooks invert the polling model — instead of your app asking 'did anything happen?', the source service pushes an HTTP POST to your URL when an event fires. They power Stripe payment confirmations, GitHub CI triggers, and Slack notifications.
words.techContext: Always verify webhook signatures to prevent spoofed events. Return 200 quickly and process asynchronously to avoid delivery timeouts.
words.exampleSentence: We processed Stripe payments via webhook so our database updated the moment the charge succeeded, not when the user navigated back.
words.etymology: coined by Jeff Lindsay in 2007 in a blog post proposing 'web hooks' as a reverse HTTP pattern.

words.shortDefinition: A function that sits in the request-response pipeline and can inspect, modify, or terminate requests.
words.longDefinition: Middleware chains functions that each receive the request, optionally transform it, then pass it to the next handler. Common uses: authentication, logging, rate limiting, compression, and CORS. The order of middleware matters — auth must run before protected routes.
words.techContext: Thinking of a web server as a middleware pipeline helps keep cross-cutting concerns (auth, logging) separate from business logic.
words.exampleSentence: We added a middleware that logged every request's duration so we could spot slow endpoints in production.
words.etymology: from mid- + software; originally described software gluing mainframes to applications (1960s), repurposed for web frameworks in the 2000s.

words.shortDefinition: A lightweight text format for representing structured data as key-value pairs and arrays.
words.longDefinition: JavaScript Object Notation is language-agnostic despite its name. It supports strings, numbers, booleans, null, objects, and arrays. JSON replaced XML as the dominant web data format due to its readability and native JavaScript compatibility.
words.techContext: Used for REST API payloads, config files, and data storage. JSON has no support for comments, dates, or binary — common gotchas.
words.exampleSentence: The API returned a JSON payload with a nested user object, but the mobile client expected a flat structure.
words.etymology: introduced and popularised by Douglas Crockford around 2001, standardised as ECMA-404 in 2013.

codeExample.code:
{
  "id": 42,
  "name": "Ada Lovelace",
  "roles": ["admin", "user"],
  "active": true,
  "metadata": null
}

words.shortDefinition: A three-digit number in an HTTP response indicating whether the request succeeded or failed.
words.longDefinition: HTTP status codes are grouped by first digit: 2xx (success), 3xx (redirect), 4xx (client error), 5xx (server error). Returning the correct code is critical — returning 200 on errors causes clients to mishandle failures.
words.techContext: Common codes every developer must know: 200 OK, 201 Created, 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found, 422 Unprocessable Entity, 429 Too Many Requests, 500 Internal Server Error.
words.exampleSentence: The client was retrying on 400 errors, which it should never do — 400 means the request itself is wrong.
words.etymology: standardised by IETF in RFC 2616 (1999), evolved through subsequent RFCs.

words.shortDefinition: Spending disproportionate time on trivial decisions while ignoring important ones.
words.longDefinition: The tendency to focus excessive attention on minor, easy-to-grasp details (like the color of a bikeshed) while avoiding complex, critical decisions (like nuclear reactor design). A productivity anti-pattern in engineering teams.
words.techContext: Invoked when teams spend too long debating minor issues like code formatting or naming conventions instead of addressing architecture or performance concerns.
words.exampleSentence: We spent two hours bikeshedding the button color instead of discussing the database migration strategy.
words.etymology: from Parkinson's Law of Triviality (1957) — hypothetical committee debates bikeshed color endlessly.

words.shortDefinition: A surface indication in code that usually signals a deeper problem in the system.
words.longDefinition: Coined by Kent Beck and popularised by Martin Fowler, a code smell is not necessarily a bug — the code may work — but it indicates structural issues that will make future change harder. Smells are a cue to investigate and likely refactor.
words.techContext: Common smells: long methods, large classes, duplicate code, feature envy, and data clumps. Identifying them is the first step to improving a codebase.
words.exampleSentence: The function had a strong code smell — it was 300 lines long and touched six different concerns.
words.etymology: coined by Kent Beck; popularised in Martin Fowler's Refactoring (1999). Source: martinfowler.com/bliki/CodeSmell.html

words.shortDefinition: The extra effort required to add new features because of earlier shortcuts taken in the codebase.
words.longDefinition: Coined by Ward Cunningham, technical debt frames quick-and-dirty code as a financial loan: you move faster now but pay interest (slower future development) until you repay it through refactoring. Not all debt is bad — a deliberate shortcut for a deadline is a considered tradeoff; reckless debt accumulates unknowingly.
words.techContext: Useful for communicating with non-engineers why cleanup work has business value. Teams track debt in backlogs and allocate capacity to repay it.
words.exampleSentence: We took on technical debt to hit the launch date, but we've budgeted two sprints next quarter to repay it.
words.etymology: metaphor coined by Ward Cunningham in 1992; expanded by Martin Fowler into a debt quadrant. Source: martinfowler.com/bliki/TechnicalDebt.html

words.shortDefinition: A principle stating you should not build a capability until you actually need it.
words.longDefinition: You Aren't Gonna Need It warns against speculative generality — adding abstractions, config options, or features for hypothetical future needs. Fowler notes that even if you do eventually need it, it's cheaper to add it then than to maintain unused code now.
words.techContext: YAGNI fights gold-plating and over-engineering. It pairs with simple design: build the simplest thing that works, then evolve it when requirements are real.
words.exampleSentence: We removed the plugin architecture we'd built speculatively — YAGNI, and it had been adding complexity for a year unused.
words.etymology: popularised by Ron Jeffries during the Extreme Programming movement (late 1990s). Source: martinfowler.com/bliki/Yagni.html

words.shortDefinition: A principle that every method should either change state or return a value, but never both.
words.longDefinition: Defined by Bertrand Meyer and popularised by Fowler, CQS states that queries return results without side effects, while commands mutate state without returning values. This makes code easier to reason about — you can call a query freely without worrying about hidden effects.
words.techContext: Violated by patterns like stack.pop() (mutates and returns) and getter-with-side-effects. CQRS at the architecture level applies the same idea across services.
words.exampleSentence: The bug was caused by a CQS violation — the isValid() method was silently caching the result in a database column.
words.etymology: coined by Bertrand Meyer in Object-Oriented Software Construction (1988). Source: martinfowler.com/bliki/CommandQuerySeparation.html

words.shortDefinition: The percentage of source code lines executed when a test suite runs.
words.longDefinition: Test coverage is useful for finding untested parts of a codebase but is a poor quality metric on its own — 100% coverage can still miss critical cases. Fowler cautions that chasing a coverage number encourages writing meaningless tests that execute code without asserting anything.
words.techContext: Use coverage to find gaps, not as a target. Branch coverage (every if/else path) is more meaningful than line coverage. 80% is a common pragmatic threshold.
words.exampleSentence: Our coverage was at 95% but we still had a production bug — the tests weren't asserting the right things.
words.etymology: metric emerged with structured testing in the 1970s; Fowler's critique at martinfowler.com/bliki/TestCoverage.html

codeExample.code:
# Run tests with coverage report
npx jest --coverage

# Output:
# Statements : 94.2%
# Branches   : 78.1%  ← the meaningful one
# Functions  : 96.5%

words.shortDefinition: A system deliberately designed to be thrown away once you understand the real problem.
words.longDefinition: Fowler argues that sometimes the best architecture you can build now is one you plan to discard in a few years once you've learned more. Companies like eBay and Twitter rebuilt their architectures multiple times. The first version isn't a failure — it's how you discover what you actually need.
words.techContext: Justifies shipping a simple solution fast rather than over-engineering for scale you don't yet have. Pairs with YAGNI and evolutionary architecture.
words.exampleSentence: We knew the monolith was sacrificial architecture — it got us to market quickly and taught us the real performance bottlenecks.
words.etymology: coined by Martin Fowler. Source: martinfowler.com/bliki/SacrificialArchitecture.html

words.shortDefinition: A code smell where the same group of data items repeatedly appear together and should be an object.
words.longDefinition: When you see the same three or four fields (e.g., street, city, postcode) passed together everywhere, that's a data clump. Fowler recommends extracting them into a class or record — if removing one field makes the others meaningless, they belong together.
words.techContext: A clue that a missing concept exists in your domain. Naming the concept often reveals important business logic that was previously implicit.
words.exampleSentence: We kept passing startDate, endDate, and timezone together everywhere — extracting them into a DateRange object cleaned up a dozen function signatures.
words.etymology: catalogued in Martin Fowler's Refactoring (1999) as one of the canonical code smells. Source: martinfowler.com/bliki/DataClump.html

words.shortDefinition: A name that communicates why something exists and what it does, without requiring a comment to explain it.
words.longDefinition: From Fowler's function length heuristic: if you have to look at code to understand what it does, extract it and name it after the 'what'. Good names make code self-documenting — the name carries the intent so the reader never needs to read the implementation to understand the call site.
words.techContext: The most impactful code quality improvement is often just better naming. Rename variables, functions, and classes before other refactoring — it clarifies what other changes are needed.
words.exampleSentence: Renaming processData() to applyDiscountRules() instantly made three call sites readable without any other changes.
words.etymology: concept from Kent Beck's Smalltalk Best Practice Patterns (1996); Fowler applies it throughout Refactoring. Source: martinfowler.com/bliki/FunctionLength.html

words.shortDefinition: Always leave the code in a better state than you found it.
words.longDefinition: Adapted from the Boy Scout motto by Robert C. Martin (Uncle Bob), the rule advocates opportunistic refactoring: whenever you touch code, make a small improvement — rename a variable, extract a function, delete a comment. Compound interest of tiny improvements keeps codebases healthy over time.
words.techContext: Fowler calls this opportunistic refactoring. It prevents the 'broken windows' effect where developers assume messy code is acceptable because it's already messy.
words.exampleSentence: The PR included the feature plus three small cleanups nearby — the author was following the boy scout rule.
words.etymology: adapted from the Boy Scouts of America motto by Robert C. Martin; Fowler covers it as Opportunistic Refactoring. Source: martinfowler.com/bliki/OpportunisticRefactoring.html

words.shortDefinition: Improving code structure without changing its behavior.
words.longDefinition: The process of restructuring existing code to make it cleaner, more maintainable, or more efficient while preserving its external behavior. A key practice in maintaining healthy codebases.
words.techContext: Discussed when improving code quality, reducing technical debt, or preparing code for new features.
words.exampleSentence: Before adding the new feature, I spent a day refactoring the authentication module.
words.etymology: coined by Martin Fowler in 1999, combining "re-" (again) + "factoring" (breaking into components).

words.shortDefinition: Independent components that don't affect each other when changed.
words.longDefinition: In software architecture, refers to system components or design decisions that can be changed independently without impacting other parts. Borrowed from mathematics where orthogonal vectors are perpendicular.
words.techContext: Used when discussing modular design, separation of concerns, and clean architecture.
words.exampleSentence: Our authentication and authorization systems are orthogonal, so we can swap auth providers without touching permissions.
words.etymology: from Greek orthos ("straight") + gōnia ("angle") — literally "right-angled."

words.shortDefinition: A named container that stores a value which can change during program execution.
words.longDefinition: A variable is a symbolic name bound to a memory location. Variables allow programs to store, retrieve, and manipulate data dynamically. Their type, scope, and mutability vary by language and declaration keyword.
words.techContext: Variable naming, scoping, and lifetime are foundational — accidental globals, shadowing, and mutation bugs all trace back to variable misunderstanding.
words.exampleSentence: The bug was caused by a variable being reassigned inside the loop, corrupting state across iterations.
words.etymology: from Latin variabilis 'changeable', from variare 'to vary'.

words.shortDefinition: A reusable, named block of code that performs a specific task and optionally returns a value.
words.longDefinition: Functions encapsulate a sequence of statements under a name so they can be invoked multiple times. They accept input through parameters and reduce duplication by centralising logic. Functions are the primary unit of abstraction in most programming languages.
words.techContext: Decomposing programs into focused functions reduces complexity, enables testing, and is the first step toward clean architecture.
words.exampleSentence: We extracted the validation logic into a separate function so it could be unit-tested independently.
words.etymology: from Latin functio 'performance, execution', from fungi 'to perform'.

words.shortDefinition: A control structure that repeats a block of code until a condition is met.
words.longDefinition: Loops allow repetitive tasks without duplicating code. Common forms include for (fixed count), while (condition-based), and for-each (collection iteration). An infinite loop occurs when the exit condition is never reached.
words.techContext: Used for iterating over data, polling, retrying, and processing streams. Nested loops often signal O(n²) complexity worth optimising.
words.exampleSentence: The performance issue traced back to a nested loop processing a 10,000-item list inside an O(n²) algorithm.
words.etymology: Old English lyppe; applied to programming's circular repetition in the 1960s.

words.shortDefinition: A technique where a function calls itself to solve progressively smaller instances of the same problem.
words.longDefinition: Recursion requires a base case (trivially solved) and a recursive case (a smaller subproblem). Each call adds a frame to the call stack; without a base case the stack overflows. Tree traversal, divide-and-conquer, and parsing are naturally recursive.
words.techContext: Elegant for tree/graph problems but carries stack-depth risk. Iterative rewrites or tail-call optimisation are common remedies.
words.exampleSentence: We replaced the recursive directory traversal with an iterative version after hitting stack overflows on deeply nested paths.
words.etymology: from Latin recursio 'a running back', from recurrere 'to run back'.

codeExample.code:
def factorial(n: int) -> int:
    if n <= 1:              # base case
        return 1
    return n * factorial(n - 1)  # recursive case

words.shortDefinition: A function that retains access to variables from the scope in which it was defined, even after that scope has closed.
words.longDefinition: Closures bundle a function with its surrounding lexical environment. They enable data encapsulation, callbacks with state, and partial application. In JavaScript, closures are the mechanism behind module patterns and many React hooks.
words.techContext: Essential for event handlers, async callbacks, and factory functions. Stale closures capturing outdated state are a common React bug.
words.exampleSentence: The counter keeps its own private count thanks to a closure — no global variable needed.
words.etymology: from Latin clausura 'a closing'; applied to programming by Peter Landin (1964) in SECD machine theory.

words.shortDefinition: A data type with exactly two values: true or false.
words.longDefinition: Booleans represent binary logic states and are the foundation of all conditional branching. They arise from comparisons, logical operators (&&, ||, !), and predicates. Most languages also have truthy/falsy coercion rules that can cause subtle bugs.
words.techContext: Boolean flags, feature toggles, and predicate functions all use boolean logic. Beware implicit coercion — '' and 0 are falsy in JS.
words.exampleSentence: The isLoading boolean flag controls whether the spinner renders, keeping UI state explicit.
words.etymology: named after George Boole (1815–1864), who formalised binary logic in Boolean algebra.

words.shortDefinition: An ordered, indexed collection of elements stored in contiguous memory.
words.longDefinition: Arrays provide O(1) random access by index and are the most fundamental data structure. In low-level languages arrays are fixed-size; high-level languages typically offer dynamic arrays (vectors) that resize automatically. Indexing usually starts at 0.
words.techContext: Arrays underlie lists, stacks, queues, and matrices. Off-by-one errors and out-of-bounds access are their most common pitfalls.
words.exampleSentence: We sorted the array of timestamps in descending order before paginating the results.
words.etymology: from Old French areer 'to arrange'; computing use from 1950s IBM manuals.

codeExample.code:
nums = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5]
nums.append(9)     # O(1) amortised
nums[0]            # 3  — O(1) access
nums.sort()        # in-place O(n log n)

words.shortDefinition: A value representing the intentional absence of any object or value.
words.longDefinition: Null signals that a variable has no value — distinct from zero, empty string, or undefined. Tony Hoare, who invented null references in 1965, later called it his 'billion-dollar mistake' because of the runtime errors null dereferences cause.
words.techContext: Null checks are ubiquitous. Modern languages address null unsafety with optionals (Swift, Kotlin) or Option types (Rust, Haskell).
words.exampleSentence: We crashed production because we called .length on a null response without a guard check.
words.etymology: from Latin nullus 'none, not any'; adopted in computing in the 1960s.

words.shortDefinition: A finite, step-by-step procedure for solving a problem or computing a result.
words.longDefinition: Algorithms are language-agnostic recipes for computation. They are evaluated by correctness, time complexity, and space complexity. Classic categories include sorting, searching, graph traversal, and dynamic programming.
words.techContext: Choosing the right algorithm can change runtime from hours to milliseconds. Big O notation measures algorithmic efficiency.
words.exampleSentence: Switching from a bubble sort algorithm to quicksort cut the data processing time from 40 seconds to under one.
words.etymology: from Latinised name of 9th-century Persian mathematician Al-Khwārizmī, whose book introduced systematic calculation.

codeExample.code:
# Binary search — O(log n)
def binary_search(arr, target):
    lo, hi = 0, len(arr) - 1
    while lo <= hi:
        mid = (lo + hi) // 2
        if arr[mid] == target: return mid
        elif arr[mid] < target: lo = mid + 1
        else: hi = mid - 1
    return -1

words.shortDefinition: The region of code where a variable or identifier is visible and accessible.
words.longDefinition: Scope determines identifier visibility and lifetime. Lexical (static) scoping — used by most modern languages — means visibility is defined by where code is written, not where it runs. Common levels: block, function, module, and global scope.
words.techContext: Scope bugs — accidental globals, variable shadowing, stale closures — are among the most common sources of hard-to-find errors.
words.exampleSentence: The variable was declared inside the if block but accessed outside it — a classic scope mistake.
words.etymology: from Greek skopos 'aim, target' — in programming, what a piece of code can 'see'.

words.shortDefinition: An operation that produces the same result no matter how many times it runs.
words.longDefinition: A property of certain operations where applying them multiple times has the same effect as applying them once. Critical for safe retries in distributed systems and HTTP methods like PUT and DELETE.
words.techContext: Commonly used in API design and distributed systems discussions.
words.exampleSentence: The retry logic is safe because the endpoint is idempotent.
words.etymology: from Latin idem ("same") + potent ("power") — coined 1870 in algebra.
